file_insights.changes_summary: Added lifecycle management methods for BroadcastReceiver registration
key_additions: unregister() method with safe unregistration guard
Comprehensive KDoc documentation for both methods

patterns_discovered.description: Both register() and unregister() check isRegistered flag to prevent double registration/unregistration
patterns_discovered.benefit: Prevents crashes and resource leaks from improper lifecycle management

patterns_discovered.description: Log statements at multiple decision points to aid debugging
patterns_discovered.location: All guard clauses and after state changes
patterns_discovered.benefit: Clear visibility into lifecycle state transitions

patterns_discovered.description: Clear KDoc comments specifying when methods should be called (onResume/onStart for register, onPause/onStop for unregister)
patterns_discovered.benefit: Prevents developer mistakes in Activity/Fragment integration

gotchas_discovered.gotcha: Memory leak vulnerability if unregister() not called
gotchas_discovered.mitigation: Documented in comments, but requires developer discipline to call unregister() in onPause/onStop

gotchas_discovered.context: If register/unregister called from different threads, isRegistered flag could be out of sync
gotchas_discovered.mitigation: Current implementation assumes single-threaded access, which is typical for Android lifecycle

gotchas_discovered.context: Android framework could throw exceptions in edge cases
gotchas_discovered.mitigation: Could be added in future iterations

approach_outcome.completion_notes: Lifecycle methods successfully implemented with proper state management and guards against improper usage

recommendations.suggestion: Ensure Activity/Fragment integration examples show proper lifecycle binding in onStart/onResume and onStop/onPause
Consider adding try-catch blocks around registerReceiver/unregisterReceiver calls to handle framework exceptions
Add unit tests verifying register() called twice doesn't double-register, and unregister() called twice doesn't crash
If multi-threaded access becomes requirement, consider using @Synchronized or atomic operations instead of simple boolean flag
The logging strategy with TAG is well-implemented; maintain this pattern in subsequent subtasks
